zh.Halberton, Devon: 英國英格蘭戴文哈爾波頓
zh.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee於大約1535年出生在英國英格蘭戴文武爾。他至少有1個兒子。他去世於1587年在英國英格蘭戴文哈爾波頓享年53歲。

zh-hans.Halberton, Devon: 英国英格兰戴文哈爾波頓
zh-hans.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee于大约1535年出生在英国英格兰戴文武爾。他至少有1个儿子。他去世于1587年在英国英格兰戴文哈爾波頓享年53岁。

nameMeanings.name: Parker
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: occupational name from Middle English parker ‘park-keeper’ (Old French parquier, parchier), an officer employed to look after deer and other game in a hunting park (see Park 1). This surname is also very common among African Americans. It has also been recorded since medieval times in Ireland.
Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish names.

variants.name: Parkman
meanings: English:

occupational name from Middle English park ‘enclosure, game preserve, park’ (Old French parc) + man, sometimes for one living at or near such a place but probably more often denoting someone employed to look after after deer and other game in a hunting park; compare Parker .
occupational name denoting the servant (Middle English man) of someone called Park (see Park 2).

variants.name: Parkerson
meanings: English: from the occupational name Parker + patronymic -son.

variants.name: Parkey
meanings: English (Cheshire and Lancashire): dialect pronunciation of Parker . Parker has a long history in Macclesfield, Manchester, and Bridgnorth.

variants.name: Park
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Young, Sang, Sung, Yong, Jung, Jong, Chan, Jae, Jin, Kwang, Kyung, Myung, Chong, Chang, Chung, Byung, Min, Moon, Chul, Hae, Jeong, Dae, Seong, Myong.

English and Scottish: from Middle English and Older Scots parc, parke, perk ‘park, enclosure’ (Old French parc), often referring to the grounds of a manor house or a deer park where the lord hunted. The name was probably synonymous with Parker , denoting an officer who looked after a park for the lord of the manor.
English and Scottish: from a medieval pet form of the personal name   Peter  . Compare   Parkin  .

nameMeanings.name: alias
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Carlos, Luis, Juan, Manuel, Jorge, Jesus, Arturo, Ramon, Pedro, Francisco, Miguel.
Spanish: from the medieval personal name Arias, of disputed origin.

Jewish (Sephardic): adoption of the Spanish surname (see above) at the moment of conversion to Roman Catholicism. After the return to Judaism (generations later), some descendants retained the name their families used as Catholics.

nameMeanings.name: Keeper
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Americanized form of German   Kuper   1.

English (London): occupational name from Middle English keper ‘guardian, warden, custodian’, or a topographic name from Middle English keper, either ‘dweller by the castle keep’ or ‘dweller by the look-out place’. See Keep for the senses.

variants.name: Kuper
meanings: North German (also Küper): occupational name for a cooper, from Middle Low German kuper ‘cooper’.
Swiss German: nickname for a sulky person, from Swiss German kupen ‘to sulk, to pout’.
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): from Yiddish kuper ‘copper’ (or a Yiddishized form of German Kupfer), hence a metonymic occupational name for a worker or trader in copper or an artificial name.

nameMeanings.name: also
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Italian: from the personal name Aldo, a short form of various compound names of ancient Germanic origin, formed with alda ‘old, wise’, for example Aldobrando, Arnaldo, Rinaldo.

variants.name: Audino
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rosario, Nicola, Salvatore.
Italian: from a pet form of the personal name Audo, a variant of Aldo .

variants.name: Audia
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Domenic, Francesco, Luigi, Santo.
Italian (Calabria): from the personal name Auda, a variant feminine form of Aldo .

variants.name: Audi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Antonio, Giuseppe, Massimo.  Arabic/Muslim Essa, Aiman, Farid, Hiam, Issam, Nabila, Naziha.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of the personal name Audo, a variant of Aldo .
Arabic (mainly Palestine): from ʿAwdī, a habitational name for someone from Al-ʿAwd, the name of several places mostly on Arabian Peninsula. It is found among both Muslims and Christians.

variants.name: Aldi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Giovanni.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of the personal name   Aldo  .

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Emile, Jacques, Andre, Marcel, Pierre, Leonce, Normand, Camille, Lucien, Gilles, Henri.

English, French, West Indian (mainly Haiti), German, and Dutch: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements rīc ‘power(ful)’ + hard ‘hardy, brave, strong’. The Middle English forms were Richard and Rikard, respectively Parisian Old French and Old Picard pronunciations of the ancient Germanic name. Middle English Rikard gives rise to the pet form Rick , whilst Richard has given the pet forms Richie and Rich . Both produced rhyming pet forms, Dick and Hick for Rikard and Hitch for Richard.

History: The LA Richard families are mainly descended from Acadian refugees in the second half of the 18th century. — This surname is listed in the register of Huguenot ancestors recognized by the Huguenot Society of America.

variants.name: Recker
meanings: North German, East Frisian, and Dutch: from a variant of the personal name   Richard  . This surname is very rare in East Frisia.
North German: topographic name from Middle Low German recke ‘stretch, route, hedge’, or a habitational name for someone from Recke, near Osnabrück.

variants.name: Ricard
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Emile, Andre, Armand, Normand, Raoul, Adelard, Adrien, Amedee, Damien, Fernand, Francois, Germaine.
French: variant of   Richard  . Compare   Recor  ,   Record  ,   Recore  , and   Ricord  .
Channel Islands (Guernsey): variant of Rickard , the characteristic Norman pronunciation of Richard.

variants.name: Ricardo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Manuel, Juan, Roberto, Carlos, Angel, Jorge, Ramon, Ediberto, Guillermo, Joaquin, Juana.
Potuguese and Spanish: from the personal name Ricardo, an equivalent of Richard .

Jewish (Sephardic): adoption of the name in 1 above at the moment of conversion to Roman Catholicism. After the return to Judaism (generations later), some descendants retained the name their families used as Catholics.

variants.name: Rick
meanings: English (Nottinghamshire and Lincolnshire): from the Middle English personal name Rick, a pet form of Richard . Compare Rich 2.
German: from a short form of any of the ancient Germanic personal names formed with rīc ‘power(ful)’.
North German: topographic name from Middle Low German ri(c)k ‘enclosed land, fence’, or from reke ‘row, strip of trees’.

variants.name: Riccardo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Salvatore, Angelo, Vito, Antonio, Carmine, Domenico, Francesca.
Italian: from the personal name Riccardo, an equivalent of Richard .

variants.name: Pritchard
meanings: Welsh: Anglicized form of Welsh ap Rhisiart ‘son of Richard’(see Richard ).

variants.name: Reick
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Otto, Helmuth.
German:
from a medieval personal name, a short form of   Richard  , or less commonly of some other compound name with this first element.

variants.name: Reichard
meanings: German: from an ancient Germanic personal name (see   Richard  ). This surname (and its variants) has also been adopted by Jews.

variants.name: Reisert
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Kurt, Mathias.
German: variant of Reissert, a variant of the ancient Germanic personal name Ricohard (see Richard ).

variants.name: Reitz
meanings: German: habitational name from a place near Stolpe in Pomerania called Reitz.
German: from a late medieval pet form of the personal name Richard or Heinrich (see Henry ).
Jewish (Ashkenazic): from the Yiddish female personal name Raytse, of ancient Germanic origin.

nameMeanings.name: Park
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Young, Sang, Sung, Yong, Jung, Jong, Chan, Jae, Jin, Kwang, Kyung, Myung, Chong, Chang, Chung, Byung, Min, Moon, Chul, Hae, Jeong, Dae, Seong, Myong.

English and Scottish: from Middle English and Older Scots parc, parke, perk ‘park, enclosure’ (Old French parc), often referring to the grounds of a manor house or a deer park where the lord hunted. The name was probably synonymous with Parker , denoting an officer who looked after a park for the lord of the manor.
English and Scottish: from a medieval pet form of the personal name   Peter  . Compare   Parkin  .

variants.name: Parker
meanings: English: occupational name from Middle English parker ‘park-keeper’ (Old French parquier, parchier), an officer employed to look after deer and other game in a hunting park (see Park 1). This surname is also very common among African Americans. It has also been recorded since medieval times in Ireland.
Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish names.

variants.name: Pak
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Chong, Yong, Young, Sung, Song, Sang, Kyong, Kwang, Chun, Chang, Sun, Jong, Jung, Chan, Chung, Byung, Nam, Myong, Pyong, Chol, Hae, Hyong, In Kyu, Tuk.

Korean: written 박, in Chinese characters 朴, meaning ‘simple’; this is the third most common surname in Korea and is found in every part of the peninsula. The Pak clans claim no founding ancestors from foreign lands - all are native to Korea. Although some sources indicate that there are 270 Pak clans, only 44 of these can be documented, and much of their early history is shrouded in myth and legend. All Pak clans spring from a single semi-mythological ancestor named Pak Hyŏkkŏse (朴 赫; 居世). Before the establishment of the Shilla kingdom the southern tip of the peninsula is said to have been ruled by six elders, leaders of the six tribes who inhabited that part of the country. In AD 69 a leader, Pak Hyŏkkŏse, arrived at the foot of a mountain in Kyŏngju in Kyŏngsang north province. Legend has it that he was inside a golden gourd-shaped egg. When the egg was opened, Pak Hyŏkkŏse emerged and, after taking a wife (who was borne on the back of a dragon), he established the new Shilla kingdom. The word pak in Korean (박; 朴) can mean either ‘gourd’ or ‘to shine brightly’, and the surname Pak is said to denote either the gourd-shaped egg in which the youth was found or the light that is said to have radiated from his head. Compare Park .
Chinese: Cantonese form of the surnames 白 and 柏, see   Bai   1 and 2.

variants.name: Parks
meanings: English:
variant of Park 1 with genitival, plural, or post-medieval excrescent -s.
variant of Perk , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Parrack
meanings: English: topographic name or nickname from Middle English par(r)o(c)k ‘enclosed pasture, paddock’ (Old English pearroc), signifying one who lived or worked at a paddock; there is sometimes later confusion with Park .

variants.name: Parke
meanings: English: variant of   Park  . This form of the surname is common in Ireland.

nameMeanings.name: alias
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Carlos, Luis, Juan, Manuel, Jorge, Jesus, Arturo, Ramon, Pedro, Francisco, Miguel.
Spanish: from the medieval personal name Arias, of disputed origin.

Jewish (Sephardic): adoption of the Spanish surname (see above) at the moment of conversion to Roman Catholicism. After the return to Judaism (generations later), some descendants retained the name their families used as Catholics.

nameMeanings.name: Lee
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sang, Jae, Jong, Jung, Sung, Yong, Kyung, Seung, Dong, Kwang, Myung.

English: topographic name for someone who lived near a meadow or a patch of arable land, Middle English lee, lea, from Old English lēa, dative case (used after a preposition) of lēah, which originally meant ‘wood or glade’.

English: habitational name from any of the many places in England named with Old English lēah ‘wood, glade’, including Lee in Buckinghamshire, Essex, Hampshire, Kent, and Shropshire, and Lea in Cheshire, Derbyshire, Herefordshire, Lancashire, Lincolnshire, and Wiltshire.

variants.name: Ley
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
German: from a medieval personal name, Eloy, from Latin Eligius (see Eligio ), a Christian saint's name.
German (Rhineland): topographic name from Middle High German leie ‘rock, stone, slate’, or a habitational name from any of several places called with this word. Compare Leier .

variants.name: Lees
meanings: English and Scottish: topographic name from Middle English lees ‘fields, arable land’, plural of lee (see Lee ), or from Middle English lese ‘pasture, meadow’ (Old English lǣs).

English: habitational name fromLeece or Lees in Lancashire, or Leese in Cheshire, all named from OldEnglish lēas ‘woodland clearings’ (plural oflēah), or from Leece in Cumbria, which was probably namedwith a Celtic word, lïss ‘hall, court, the principalhouse in a district’.
English: variant of   Leece   1.

variants.name: Lay
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Scottish: shortened form of   McClay  .
German: habitational name from any of the places so named, in the Rhineland near Koblenz and in Bavaria, from lay(h), a word meaning ‘stone, rock, slate’.

variants.name: Laye
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 李, based on its Cantonese pronunciation. See   Li   1.

variants.name: Lie
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Oystein, Sven, Thor.  German Hans, Gunther, Hilde.  Korean Han, Chang Ho, Chen, Chun, Hok, Jung, Kam, Liang, Lie, Ling, Shuen, Yung.
Norwegian: variant of Li 11, a habitational name from any of numerous farmsteads named Li, from Old Norse hlíth ‘mountain slope, hillside’. In Norway this is the most frequent spelling.
German: unexplained.

variants.name: Layman
meanings: English: topographic name for a dweller by a woodland clearing or a meadow, from Middle English ley + man. Compare Lee .
English: nickname from layman, a word denoting an uneducated person.
Americanized form of German   Lehmann  .

variants.name: Lea
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surnames 李 and 利, see   Li   1 and 3.

variants.name: Lye
meanings: English: variant of   Lee   1, a topographic name for someone who lived near a patch of arable land, or a habitational name from Lye in Herefordshire (with the same etymology).

Norwegian: habitational name from a farmstead in Rogaland named Lye, Old Norse Lýgi meaning ‘alliance, covenant’, used to denote a place sanctified by such an agreement, such as a court or council meeting place.
Altered form of German   Ley  .

variants.name: Saelee
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Chan, Kao, Nai, Chai, Cheng, Chien, Foo, Hin, Muang, Pao, Seng, Song, Sou.
Southeast Asian (Iu Mien): from the name of the Lee clan of the Iu Mien people from Laos + the Thai prefix sae, denoting a clan. Compare Lee 8.

variants.name: Leigh
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places (in at least sixteen counties, but especially Leigh in Lancashire) named either with the nominative case of Old English lēah ‘woodland clearing’ (see Lee ) or with lēage, a late dative form of this word (see Lye ).
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 李, based on its Cantonese pronunciation. See   Li   1.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

nameMeanings.name: Worsley
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Lancashire): habitational name from Worsley in Lancashire or Worsley Farm in Rock (Worcestershire). The Lancashire placename may derive from an Old English personal name Weorcgȳth (genitive Weorcgȳthes) + Old English lēah ‘wood, woodland clearing’. The Worcestershire placename may take its name from a lost stream-name Worf + Old English lēah.

nameMeanings.name: father
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: 1 English: nickname from Middle English fader, father, feder, fether (Old English fæder) ‘father’, often used to denote someone who exercised protecting care like that of a father. Some examples may belong at (2).
2 English: relationship name from the Middle English personal name Fader (Old Danish Fathir, Old Swedish Fadhir). Some early bearers may belong with (1).

variants.name: Feather
meanings: From the vocabulary word denoting the hardy, brightly coloured plant (Middle English hather; the spelling was altered in the 18th century as a result of folk etymological association with heath). The name was first used in the late 19th century and became particularly popular from the mid-1940s.

variants.name: Fathers
meanings: English: variant of Father with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.

en.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee was born about 1535, in Uffculme, Devon, England, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 son. He died in 1587, in Halberton, Devon, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 53.

fr.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee est né environ 1535, à Uffculme, Devon, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Il a eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé en 1587, à Halberton, Devon, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 53 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee wurde um 1535 in Uffculme, Devon, England, United Kingdom geboren. Er hatte mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1587 im Alter von 53 Jahren in Halberton, Devon, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee è nato il circa 1535 nel Uffculme, Devon, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio. Morì nel 1587, nel Halberton, Devon, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 53 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee(은)는 대략 1535년 영국 잉글랜드 데폰 프컬에서 태어났다. 그는 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1587년 영국 잉글랜드 데폰 할버턴에서 53세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee nasceu aproximadamente 1535, em Uffculme, Devon, Inglaterra, United Kingdom. Ele teve pelo menos 1 filho. Ele faleceu em 1587, em Halberton, Devon, Inglaterra, United Kingdom, com 53 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee родился около 1535 г. в местности уффкулм, девон, Англия, Великобритания. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын. Он умер в 1587 г. в местности хэлбиртон, девон, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 53 лет.

es.lifeSummary: Richard   Worsley father Parker   alias Keeper also Richard Park alias Lee nació aproximadamente 1535, en Uffculme, Devon, Inglaterra, Reino Unido. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hijo. Murió en 1587, en Halberton, Devon, Inglaterra, Reino Unido, a la edad de 53 años.
